description: Using the Stream trait to process an async sequence of values with combinators like map and filter.
whenToUse: When consuming a paginated API, WebSocket messages, or any async iterator of values.
whyItMatters: Streams provide a composable abstraction over async sequences, similar to iterators for sync code.

description: Using tokio::sync::Mutex to protect shared state across async tasks without blocking the runtime.
whenToUse: When multiple async tasks need exclusive access to shared mutable data across await points.
whyItMatters: Async mutexes yield to the runtime while waiting, unlike std::sync::Mutex which blocks the thread.

description: Using a CancellationToken or signal handler to gracefully shut down async tasks.
whenToUse: When a server needs to finish in-flight requests before exiting on SIGTERM.
whyItMatters: Graceful shutdown prevents data loss and connection resets during deployments.

description: Implementing exponential backoff retry logic for async operations that may transiently fail.
whenToUse: When calling external services that may return temporary errors like rate limits or network timeouts.
whyItMatters: Retry with backoff is essential for resilient distributed systems that must tolerate partial failures.

description: Moving data between general-purpose registers using the MOV instruction.
whenToUse: When you need to copy a value from one register to another for computation or argument passing.
whyItMatters: MOV is the most fundamental x86-64 instruction; understanding register transfers is the basis of all assembly.
content:
section .text
global _start

_start:
    mov rax, 42          ; load immediate into rax
    mov rbx, rax         ; copy rax to rbx
    mov rcx, rbx         ; copy rbx to rcx
    mov rdx, 0           ; zero out rdx
    mov rdx, rcx         ; copy rcx to rdx

description: Performing addition, subtraction, and multiplication using register operands.
whenToUse: When computing arithmetic expressions that operate on integer values in registers.
whyItMatters: Arithmetic instructions form the core of computation; understanding flags they set is essential for branching.
content:
section .text

add_multiply:
    mov rax, 10          ; rax = 10
    mov rbx, 20          ; rbx = 20
    add rax, rbx         ; rax = rax + rbx = 30
    sub rax, 5           ; rax = 30 - 5 = 25
    imul rax, rax, 2     ; rax = 25 * 2 = 50
    ret

description: Using AND, OR, XOR, and shift instructions for bit manipulation in registers.
whenToUse: When implementing flags, masks, hashing, or low-level protocol encoding.
whyItMatters: Bitwise operations are the fastest way to manipulate individual bits, used heavily in systems programming.
content:
section .text

bit_ops:
    mov rax, 0xFF        ; rax = 11111111b
    and rax, 0x0F        ; rax = 00001111b (mask lower nibble)
    or  rax, 0x30        ; rax = 00111111b (set bits)
    xor rbx, rbx         ; rbx = 0 (fast zero idiom)
    shl rax, 4           ; shift left by 4 (multiply by 16)
    shr rax, 2           ; shift right by 2 (divide by 4)
    ret

description: Using CMP and TEST to set CPU flags for conditional operations without storing a result.
whenToUse: When you need to check conditions before a conditional jump or conditional move.
whyItMatters: CMP and TEST set the FLAGS register which controls all conditional execution in x86-64.
content:
section .text

check_value:
    mov rax, [rdi]       ; load value from pointer
    cmp rax, 100         ; compare rax with 100
    jg  .above           ; jump if rax > 100
    test rax, rax        ; test if rax is zero
    jz  .is_zero         ; jump if zero flag set
    ret
.above:
    mov rax, 1
    ret
.is_zero:
    mov rax, -1
    ret

description: Using CMOVcc instructions to conditionally move data without branching, avoiding pipeline stalls.
whenToUse: When implementing branchless code for performance-critical paths like sorting comparisons.
whyItMatters: CMOV avoids branch misprediction penalties, which can cost 15-20 cycles on modern CPUs.
content:
section .text

; return max(rdi, rsi)
max_val:
    mov rax, rdi         ; assume rdi is max
    cmp rdi, rsi         ; compare the two values
    cmovl rax, rsi       ; if rdi < rsi, use rsi instead
    ret

description: Using LEA to compute addresses or perform arithmetic without touching memory.
whenToUse: When you need multi-operand arithmetic like base+index*scale+offset in a single instruction.
whyItMatters: LEA is a powerful one-instruction way to compute complex expressions without setting flags.
content:
section .text

lea_tricks:
    ; rax = rdi * 5  (rdi + rdi*4)
    lea rax, [rdi + rdi*4]
    ; rax = rdi * 3 + 7
    lea rax, [rdi + rdi*2 + 7]
    ; rax = rsi + rdi*8  (array indexing)
    lea rax, [rsi + rdi*8]
    ret

description: Using XCHG to atomically swap two register values in a single instruction.
whenToUse: When you need to swap two values without a temporary register or for atomic memory operations.
whyItMatters: XCHG with a memory operand has an implicit LOCK prefix, making it useful for lock-free algorithms.
content:
section .text

swap_regs:
    mov rax, 10          ; rax = 10
    mov rbx, 20          ; rbx = 20
    xchg rax, rbx        ; rax = 20, rbx = 10
    ret

; XOR swap without XCHG
xor_swap:
    xor rax, rbx         ; rax = rax ^ rbx
    xor rbx, rax         ; rbx = original rax
    xor rax, rbx         ; rax = original rbx
    ret

description: Using MOVZX and MOVSX to widen smaller values into larger registers with zero or sign extension.
whenToUse: When loading byte or word values into 64-bit registers for arithmetic that requires the full width.
whyItMatters: Incorrect extension causes subtle bugs when mixing data sizes, especially with signed values.
content:
section .text

extend_ops:
    movzx rax, byte [rdi]    ; zero-extend byte to 64 bits
    movzx rbx, word [rdi+1]  ; zero-extend word to 64 bits
    movsx rcx, byte [rdi]    ; sign-extend byte to 64 bits
    movsx rdx, dword [rdi]   ; sign-extend dword to 64 bits
    ret

description: Using PUSH and POP to save and restore register values on the stack.
whenToUse: When a function needs to preserve callee-saved registers before using them for local computation.
whyItMatters: Correct push/pop pairing maintains the call stack integrity that the entire program depends on.
content:
section .text

my_function:
    push rbp             ; save base pointer
    push rbx             ; save callee-saved register
    push r12             ; save callee-saved register
    ; ... function body uses rbx and r12 ...
    pop r12              ; restore in reverse order
    pop rbx
    pop rbp
    ret

description: Creating a standard stack frame with base pointer for local variable access and debugging.
whenToUse: When a function needs local variables on the stack and you want debugger-friendly stack traces.
whyItMatters: Stack frames enable debuggers to walk the call chain and are required by many calling conventions.
content:
section .text

function_with_locals:
    push rbp             ; save old base pointer
    mov  rbp, rsp        ; set new base pointer
    sub  rsp, 32         ; allocate 32 bytes for locals
    mov  [rbp-8], rdi    ; store first arg as local
    mov  [rbp-16], rsi   ; store second arg as local
    ; ... function body ...
    mov  rsp, rbp        ; deallocate locals
    pop  rbp             ; restore old base pointer
    ret

description: Allocating and accessing local variables using negative offsets from the base pointer.
whenToUse: When a function needs temporary storage that does not fit in registers.
whyItMatters: Understanding stack-based locals is essential for reading compiler output and debugging crashes.
content:
section .text

compute:
    push rbp
    mov  rbp, rsp
    sub  rsp, 16         ; space for two 8-byte locals
    mov  qword [rbp-8], 0    ; local_a = 0
    mov  qword [rbp-16], 0   ; local_b = 0
    mov  [rbp-8], rdi        ; local_a = first argument
    add  rax, [rbp-8]        ; use local_a
    leave                    ; mov rsp,rbp; pop rbp
    ret

description: Ensuring the stack pointer is 16-byte aligned before calling other functions, as required by the System V ABI.
whenToUse: Before any CALL instruction to prevent segfaults from SSE instructions that require aligned memory.
whyItMatters: Misaligned stacks cause crashes in library functions that use SSE/AVX instructions internally.
content:
section .text

call_printf:
    push rbp
    mov  rbp, rsp
    and  rsp, -16        ; align stack to 16 bytes
    sub  rsp, 16         ; allocate aligned space
    lea  rdi, [rel fmt]  ; first arg: format string
    mov  rsi, 42         ; second arg: value
    xor  eax, eax        ; zero float args count
    call printf
    leave
    ret

section .data
fmt: db "Value: %d", 10, 0

description: Preserving callee-saved registers (rbx, r12-r15, rbp) across function calls per the System V ABI.
whenToUse: When your function uses registers that the caller expects to be preserved across the call.
whyItMatters: Violating the calling convention corrupts the caller's state, causing hard-to-diagnose bugs.
content:
section .text
process_array:           ; rdi=array, rsi=length
    push rbx
    push r12
    mov  rbx, rdi
    mov  r12, rsi
.loop:
    test r12, r12
    jz   .done
    mov  rdi, [rbx]
    call process_element
    add  rbx, 8
    dec  r12
    jmp  .loop
.done:
    pop  r12
    pop  rbx
    ret

description: Using the 128-byte red zone below RSP for leaf function locals without adjusting the stack pointer.
whenToUse: In leaf functions that do not call other functions and need small amounts of temporary storage.
whyItMatters: The red zone saves the SUB/ADD RSP instructions, making leaf functions faster and smaller.
content:
section .text

; Leaf function: no calls, uses red zone
leaf_compute:
    mov  [rsp-8], rdi    ; store in red zone
    mov  [rsp-16], rsi   ; store in red zone
    mov  rax, [rsp-8]
    add  rax, [rsp-16]
    imul rax, rax, 3
    ret                  ; no stack frame needed

description: Using REP-prefixed string instructions for fast block memory operations.
whenToUse: When copying or filling large blocks of memory, similar to memcpy or memset.
whyItMatters: REP MOVSB/STOSB are optimized by modern CPUs with fast-string microcode, rivaling hand-tuned loops.
content:
section .text

; memcpy: rdi=dest, rsi=src, rdx=count
my_memcpy:
    mov  rcx, rdx        ; rcx = byte count
    rep  movsb           ; copy rcx bytes from [rsi] to [rdi]
    ret

; memset: rdi=dest, rsi=byte value, rdx=count
my_memset:
    mov  rax, rsi        ; al = fill byte
    mov  rcx, rdx        ; rcx = byte count
    rep  stosb           ; fill rcx bytes at [rdi] with al
    ret

description: Allocating a temporary buffer on the stack by decrementing the stack pointer.
whenToUse: When you need a short-lived buffer for string formatting or temporary data without heap allocation.
whyItMatters: Stack allocation is nearly free (just a pointer decrement) and automatically cleaned up on return.
content:
section .text

buffer_example:
    push rbp
    mov  rbp, rsp
    sub  rsp, 256        ; allocate 256-byte buffer
    lea  rdi, [rbp-256]  ; pointer to buffer
    mov  rsi, 256        ; buffer size
    call read_input      ; fill the buffer
    lea  rdi, [rbp-256]  ; pass buffer to processor
    call process_data
    leave
    ret

description: Using JMP for unconditional jumps and Jcc instructions for conditional branching based on flags.
whenToUse: When implementing if-else logic, loops, or switch statements in assembly.
whyItMatters: Conditional jumps are how all high-level control flow is implemented at the machine level.
content:
section .text

abs_value:
    mov  rax, rdi        ; rax = input
    test rax, rax        ; check sign
    jns  .positive       ; jump if not signed (positive)
    neg  rax             ; negate if negative
.positive:
    ret

description: Implementing an if-else branch using CMP and conditional jumps with a shared exit label.
whenToUse: When translating a two-branch conditional expression into assembly.
whyItMatters: Understanding if-else in assembly helps you read compiler output and optimize hot paths.
content:
section .text

; if (rdi >= 18) return 1; else return 0;
check_age:
    cmp  rdi, 18         ; compare age with 18
    jge  .adult          ; jump if greater or equal
    ; else branch
    xor  eax, eax        ; return 0
    jmp  .done
.adult:
    mov  eax, 1          ; return 1
.done:
    ret

description: Implementing a counted loop using a register as an index and decrementing to zero.
whenToUse: When iterating a known number of times, like processing array elements.
whyItMatters: Counted loops are the most common loop pattern and map directly to for-loops in high-level languages.
content:
section .text

; sum array: rdi=array ptr, rsi=count
sum_array:
    xor  rax, rax        ; sum = 0
    xor  rcx, rcx        ; index = 0
.loop:
    cmp  rcx, rsi        ; index < count?
    jge  .done
    add  rax, [rdi + rcx*8]  ; sum += array[index]
    inc  rcx             ; index++
    jmp  .loop
.done:
    ret

description: Implementing a while loop where the condition is checked at the top of each iteration.
whenToUse: When the loop body should execute zero or more times based on a runtime condition.
whyItMatters: While loops are the natural pattern for processing until a sentinel value or condition is met.
content:
section .text

; strlen: rdi = null-terminated string pointer
strlen:
    xor  rax, rax        ; length = 0
.loop:
    cmp  byte [rdi + rax], 0  ; check for null terminator
    je   .done           ; exit if found
    inc  rax             ; length++
    jmp  .loop           ; continue
.done:
    ret                  ; return length in rax

description: Using CALL and RET to implement function invocation with the System V AMD64 calling convention.
whenToUse: When structuring code into reusable functions that pass arguments in registers.
whyItMatters: The calling convention governs all inter-function communication in x86-64 binaries.
content:
section .text

; int add(int a, int b) -- args in rdi, rsi
add:
    lea  rax, [rdi + rsi]  ; rax = a + b
    ret

main:
    mov  rdi, 10         ; first argument
    mov  rsi, 20         ; second argument
    call add             ; pushes return addr, jumps to add
    ; rax now holds 30
    ret

description: Implementing a switch statement using an indirect jump through a table of label addresses.
whenToUse: When handling dense integer cases where a direct lookup is faster than a chain of comparisons.
whyItMatters: Jump tables give O(1) dispatch for switch statements, which compilers emit for dense case ranges.
content:
section .text

switch_handler:
    cmp  rdi, 3          ; bounds check
    ja   .default
    lea  rax, [rel .table]
    movsxd rcx, dword [rax + rdi*4]
    add  rcx, rax
    jmp  rcx
.table:
    dd .case0 - .table
    dd .case1 - .table
    dd .case2 - .table
    dd .case3 - .table
.case0: mov eax, 10; ret
.case1: mov eax, 20; ret
.case2: mov eax, 30; ret
.case3: mov eax, 40; ret

description: Implementing a loop that executes the body at least once with the condition check at the bottom.
whenToUse: When the loop body must run at least once, like reading input until a valid value is received.
whyItMatters: Do-while is often more efficient than while because it has one fewer jump per iteration.
content:
section .text

; count trailing zeros in rdi
ctz:
    xor  ecx, ecx        ; count = 0
.loop:
    test rdi, 1          ; check lowest bit
    jnz  .done           ; exit if bit is set
    shr  rdi, 1          ; shift right
    inc  ecx             ; count++
    jmp  .loop
.done:
    mov  eax, ecx        ; return count
    ret

description: Implementing nested loops using separate counter registers for the outer and inner iterations.
whenToUse: When processing 2D data structures like matrices or implementing algorithms with O(n^2) loops.
whyItMatters: Nested loops require careful register allocation to avoid clobbering outer counters.
content:
section .text
matrix_sum:              ; rdi=base, rsi=rows, rdx=cols
    xor  rax, rax
    xor  r8, r8
.row: cmp  r8, rsi
    jge  .end
    xor  r9, r9
.col: cmp  r9, rdx
    jge  .nxt
    imul r10, r8, rdx
    add  r10, r9
    add  rax, [rdi+r10*8]
    inc  r9
    jmp  .col
.nxt: inc  r8
    jmp  .row
.end: ret

description: Using the SYSCALL instruction to invoke the Linux write system call to print to stdout.
whenToUse: When you need to output text to the console without linking to libc.
whyItMatters: System calls are the only way user-space code can interact with the kernel for I/O and process control.
content:
section .data
    msg db "Hello, World!", 10
    len equ $ - msg

section .text
global _start

_start:
    mov  rax, 1          ; sys_write
    mov  rdi, 1          ; fd = stdout
    lea  rsi, [rel msg]  ; buffer address
    mov  rdx, len        ; byte count
    syscall
    mov  rax, 60         ; sys_exit
    xor  rdi, rdi        ; exit code 0
    syscall

description: Using the SYSCALL instruction to read input from stdin into a stack buffer.
whenToUse: When you need to accept user input or read from a file descriptor without libc.
whyItMatters: Understanding read syscalls is fundamental to building any interactive program at the lowest level.
content:
section .text
global _start

_start:
    sub  rsp, 128        ; allocate input buffer
    mov  rax, 0          ; sys_read
    mov  rdi, 0          ; fd = stdin
    mov  rsi, rsp        ; buffer on stack
    mov  rdx, 128        ; max bytes to read
    syscall              ; rax = bytes read
    ; rax contains number of bytes read
    add  rsp, 128
    mov  rax, 60         ; sys_exit
    xor  rdi, rdi
    syscall

description: Using open and close system calls to manage file descriptors for file I/O.
whenToUse: When implementing file reading or writing without relying on the C standard library.
whyItMatters: File descriptor management is the Unix foundation for all I/O, including sockets and pipes.
content:
section .data
    fname db "/tmp/test.txt", 0

section .text

open_file:
    mov  rax, 2          ; sys_open
    lea  rdi, [rel fname]; filename
    mov  rsi, 0          ; O_RDONLY
    mov  rdx, 0          ; mode (unused for read)
    syscall              ; rax = file descriptor
    mov  rbx, rax        ; save fd
    ; ... read from fd ...
    mov  rax, 3          ; sys_close
    mov  rdi, rbx        ; fd to close
    syscall
    ret

description: Terminating the process with an exit code using the exit system call.
whenToUse: At the end of every standalone assembly program to properly return control to the operating system.
whyItMatters: Failing to call exit causes the CPU to execute garbage memory, resulting in a segfault.
content:
section .text
global _start

_start:
    ; program logic here
    mov  rax, 60         ; sys_exit (Linux x86-64)
    mov  rdi, 0          ; exit status = 0 (success)
    syscall              ; invoke kernel
    ; execution never reaches here

description: Using the mmap system call to allocate a page-aligned block of memory from the kernel.
whenToUse: When you need heap memory without malloc, or want to allocate executable pages for JIT compilation.
whyItMatters: mmap is the fundamental memory allocation primitive that malloc and all allocators are built on.
content:
section .text

alloc_page:
    mov  rax, 9          ; sys_mmap
    xor  rdi, rdi        ; addr = NULL (kernel chooses)
    mov  rsi, 4096       ; length = 1 page
    mov  rdx, 3          ; PROT_READ | PROT_WRITE
    mov  r10, 0x22       ; MAP_PRIVATE | MAP_ANONYMOUS
    mov  r8, -1          ; fd = -1 (no file)
    xor  r9, r9          ; offset = 0
    syscall              ; rax = pointer to mapped memory
    ret

description: Using the fork system call to create a child process and branching on the return value.
whenToUse: When implementing process creation, daemon patterns, or parallel computation at the OS level.
whyItMatters: Fork is the Unix process creation primitive that underpins all multiprocessing.
content:
section .text

fork_example:
    mov  rax, 57         ; sys_fork
    syscall              ; returns: 0 in child, pid in parent
    test rax, rax
    jz   .child          ; if rax == 0, we are the child
    ; parent process
    mov  rdi, rax        ; child pid
    jmp  .done
.child:
    ; child process
    mov  rax, 60
    mov  rdi, 42         ; exit with code 42
    syscall
.done:
    ret

description: Using the brk system call to extend the program's data segment for simple heap allocation.
whenToUse: When implementing a simple allocator that grows memory linearly without the complexity of mmap.
whyItMatters: brk is the simplest heap growth mechanism and helps understand how early malloc implementations worked.
content:
section .text

simple_alloc:
    ; get current break
    mov  rax, 12         ; sys_brk
    xor  rdi, rdi        ; arg=0 means query current break
    syscall              ; rax = current break address
    mov  rbx, rax        ; save current break
    ; extend by 1024 bytes
    lea  rdi, [rax + 1024]
    mov  rax, 12         ; sys_brk
    syscall              ; rax = new break
    mov  rax, rbx        ; return old break as pointer
    ret

description: Checking the return value of a system call for errors, which are indicated by negative values.
whenToUse: After every system call to detect failures and handle them gracefully.
whyItMatters: Ignoring syscall errors leads to silent failures like writing to closed file descriptors.
content:
section .text

safe_write:
    mov  rax, 1          ; sys_write
    mov  rdi, rdi        ; fd (passed as argument)
    mov  rsi, rsi        ; buffer (passed as argument)
    mov  rdx, rdx        ; count (passed as argument)
    syscall
    test rax, rax        ; check for error
    js   .error          ; negative = error
    ret                  ; rax = bytes written
.error:
    neg  rax             ; convert to positive errno
    ret                  ; return negative error code

description: Accessing memory at a fixed address stored in the data section using a label.
whenToUse: When reading or writing global variables or constants whose address is known at assemble time.
whyItMatters: Direct addressing is the simplest memory access mode and is used for all global and static data.
content:
section .data
    counter dq 0         ; 64-bit global variable

section .text

increment:
    mov  rax, [rel counter]  ; load global value
    inc  rax                 ; increment
    mov  [rel counter], rax  ; store back
    ret

description: Accessing memory at the address held in a register, the most common addressing mode for pointer dereferencing.
whenToUse: When following a pointer passed as a function argument or iterating through data structures.
whyItMatters: Register indirect is the fundamental pointer dereference operation used in every non-trivial program.
content:
section .text

; dereference and double the value at pointer rdi
double_value:
    mov  rax, [rdi]      ; load value at address in rdi
    shl  rax, 1          ; double it
    mov  [rdi], rax      ; store back through pointer
    ret

description: Accessing struct fields or fixed offsets from a base pointer using register plus displacement.
whenToUse: When reading fields from a C struct where each field is at a known offset from the struct pointer.
whyItMatters: Base+offset maps directly to struct field access in C, making it essential for ABI interoperability.
content:
section .text

; struct Point { int64 x; int64 y; int64 z; }
; rdi = pointer to Point
get_magnitude_sq:
    mov  rax, [rdi]      ; x at offset 0
    imul rax, rax        ; x*x
    mov  rcx, [rdi+8]    ; y at offset 8
    imul rcx, rcx        ; y*y
    add  rax, rcx        ; x*x + y*y
    mov  rcx, [rdi+16]   ; z at offset 16
    imul rcx, rcx        ; z*z
    add  rax, rcx        ; x*x + y*y + z*z
    ret

description: Using the SIB (Scale-Index-Base) addressing mode to index into arrays with element size scaling.
whenToUse: When accessing elements of an array where the element size is 1, 2, 4, or 8 bytes.
whyItMatters: SIB addressing computes array element addresses in a single instruction, matching how compilers access arrays.
content:
section .text

; int64 array access: rdi=base, rsi=index
array_get:
    mov  rax, [rdi + rsi*8]  ; load array[index] (8 bytes each)
    ret

; int32 array access: rdi=base, rsi=index
array_get32:
    movsxd rax, dword [rdi + rsi*4]  ; load and sign-extend
    ret

; sum two array elements
sum_elements:
    mov  rax, [rdi + rsi*8]  ; array[i]
    add  rax, [rdi + rdx*8]  ; + array[j]
    ret

description: Using instruction-pointer-relative addressing for position-independent access to global data.
whenToUse: When writing position-independent code (PIC) required for shared libraries and ASLR.
whyItMatters: RIP-relative addressing is mandatory for 64-bit shared libraries and enables ASLR security.
content:
section .data
    value dq 42
    message db "hello", 0

section .text

load_globals:
    mov  rax, [rel value]    ; RIP-relative load
    lea  rdi, [rel message]  ; RIP-relative address
    ret

description: Using LEA to compute an effective address without performing a memory access.
whenToUse: When you need the address of a memory location rather than its contents, like getting a pointer to a local.
whyItMatters: LEA is used by compilers for pointer arithmetic and passing addresses of stack variables to functions.
content:
section .text

get_addresses:
    push rbp
    mov  rbp, rsp
    sub  rsp, 32
    ; compute address of local variable at rbp-16
    lea  rdi, [rbp-16]   ; rdi = pointer to local
    call init_struct      ; pass address to function
    ; compute array address: base + index * 8 + 16
    lea  rax, [rbx + rcx*8 + 16]
    leave
    ret

description: Computing the linear offset for a two-dimensional array access using row-major order.
whenToUse: When accessing elements of a 2D array stored as a flat buffer in row-major layout.
whyItMatters: Understanding 2D indexing is critical for matrix operations and image processing at the assembly level.
content:
section .text

; matrix[row][col]: rdi=base, rsi=row, rdx=col, rcx=cols_per_row
matrix_get:
    imul rsi, rcx        ; row * cols_per_row
    add  rsi, rdx        ; + col = linear index
    mov  rax, [rdi + rsi*8]  ; load element
    ret

matrix_set:
    imul rsi, rcx        ; row * cols_per_row
    add  rsi, rdx        ; + col
    mov  [rdi + rsi*8], r8   ; store value from r8
    ret

description: Following a chain of pointers by loading each pointer and dereferencing it in sequence.
whenToUse: When traversing linked data structures like linked lists, trees, or nested struct pointers.
whyItMatters: Pointer chasing is a common bottleneck due to cache misses; understanding it helps optimize data layouts.
content:
section .text

; struct Node { Node* next; int64 value; }
; traverse linked list: rdi = head pointer
list_sum:
    xor  rax, rax        ; sum = 0
.loop:
    test rdi, rdi        ; null check
    jz   .done
    add  rax, [rdi+8]    ; sum += node->value
    mov  rdi, [rdi]      ; rdi = node->next
    jmp  .loop
.done:
    ret
